hints.to seek: 'to seek' is 'quaerere' — 'quaesumus' comes from 'quaesumus' (an archaic form meaning 'we beseech')

hints.Present subjunctive: 'quaesumus' is an archaic present indicative form — the subjunctive is not used here
hints.Perfect indicative: Perfect 1st pl. would show a different stem — 'quaesumus' is present tense
hints.Imperfect indicative: Imperfect would show a longer form — 'quaesumus' is present tense

hints.quaero, quaerere, quaesivi, quaesitum: These are the principal parts of 'quaerere' (to seek) — 'quaesumus' is from the archaic 'quaeso'
hints.rogo, rogare, rogavi, rogatum: These are the principal parts of 'rogare' (to ask)
hints.peto, petere, petivi, petitum: These are the principal parts of 'petere' (to seek/aim for)

steps.target: to come to aid / to help
choices: to come to aid / to help

hints.to come: 'to come' is 'venire' — 'subveni' is from 'subvenire' (to come to aid)
hints.to conquer: 'to conquer' is 'vincere' — 'subveni' is from 'subvenire' (to come to aid)
hints.to serve: 'to serve' is 'servire' — 'subveni' is from 'subvenire' (to come to aid)

hints.Indicative: Indicative present would be 'subvenis' or 'subvenit' — 'subveni' is the imperative singular
hints.Subjunctive: Subjunctive would be 'subvenias' — 'subveni' is the imperative singular
hints.Infinitive: Infinitive would be 'subvenire' — 'subveni' is the imperative singular

hints.venio, venire, veni, ventum: These are the principal parts of 'venire' (to come) — 'subveni' is from the compound 'subvenire'
hints.servo, servare, servavi, servatum: These are the principal parts of 'servare' (to save/keep)
hints.vinco, vincere, vici, victum: These are the principal parts of 'vincere' (to conquer)

hints.Nominative: Nominative plural of 'famulus' would be 'famuli' — 'famulis' has the -is dative/ablative plural ending
hints.Accusative: Accusative plural of 'famulus' would be 'famulos' — 'famulis' is dative plural here
hints.Ablative: While 'famulis' could be ablative plural, with 'subveni' it takes a dative complement

hints.1st declension: 1st declension nouns have genitive in -ae — 'famuli' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension nouns have genitive in -is — 'famuli' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension nouns have genitive in -ūs — 'famuli' ends in -i

translation: We therefore beseech you, come to the aid of your servants
